title: Jack Corrigan
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Itן¿½s my shout this time, boys, so come along and breast the bar,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And kindly mention what youן¿½re going to take;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I donן¿½t feel extra thirsty, so Iן¿½ll sample that ן¿½three-starן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now, lad! come, look alive, for goodness sake.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So spake he, as he raised the brimming glass towards the light;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So spake ן¿½Long Jack,ן¿½ the boldest mountaineer
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who ever down from Nungar raced a ן¿½brumbyן¿½ mob in flight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or laid a stockwhip on a stubborn steer.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From Jindabyne to Providence along the Eucumbene
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The kindest-hearted fellow to be found;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when he crossed the saddle not a horse was ever seen
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That could make Jack quit his hold to seek the ground.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The women smiled with pleasure, the children laughed aloud,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The very dogs came barking at his feet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While outside the ן¿½Squatterן¿½s Armsן¿½ the men came forward in a crowd
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To welcome Jack when he rode up the street.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But though the boldest horseman who by midnight or by day
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Eן¿½er held a mob of cattle on a camp,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There were squatters on Monaro, who had yet been known to say
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That Jack was an unmitigated scamp.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And true it is Jack Corrigan possessed a serious fault
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which caused his gentle, blue-eyed wife much grief,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And many were the bitter tears she mingled with the salt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With which she cured their neighboursן¿½ tendן¿½rest beef.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And often would she tearful take her smiling spouse to task,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whoן¿½d answer, as her pretty face he kissed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That a beast lost all identity when pickled in the cask,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a bullock more or less would neן¿½er be missed.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But now as Jack stood all prepared to toss his nobbler down,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A softly-murmured whisper met his ear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I just saw Trooper Fraser get a warrant up the town,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½s after you, old man: youן¿½d better clear!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Jack never thanked the donor of this excellent advice,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As the glass fell through his fingers with a crash.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With a bound across the footpath, he was mounted in a trice
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And speeding down the roadway like a flash,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While Trooper William Fraser wore a very gloomy face,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As he watched his prey go flying down the road.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he settled in the saddle and prepared to give him chase,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As Jack struck out a line for his abode.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the road toward the Show Ground, then, there hung a big swing-gate,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Jackן¿½s filly cleared its bars in glorious style,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he held her well together, for he knew the trooperן¿½s weight
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would give him distance in each mile;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Jack rode twelve stone fully, while Bill Fraser rode but nine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sweetbriarן¿½s strength must surely soon be spent,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Being grass-fed, while the trooperן¿½s chestnut horse could always dine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Off oats and barley to his heartן¿½s content.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all aloud Jack cursed the day heן¿½d ever killed a beast
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or branded calf he couldnן¿½t call his own,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While the hoof-strokes on the road beat out a song that never ceased
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To echo in his ears with mocking tone.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Three years in gaol, in gaol three years,ן¿½ the jeering echoes sang;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The granite boulders caught the wild refrain.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A broken life, a weeping wife,ן¿½ ן¿½Twas thus the rhythm rang,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And a baby boy youן¿½ll never see againן¿½,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He groaned, and then, to dull the sound, spoke loudly to the mare,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bade her never slacken in her speed.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For Godן¿½s sake take me home, lass, with a little time to spare;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Five minutes, at the most, is all I need,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just time to catch old Dandy, where heן¿½s munching second growth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of hay; just time to leap upon his back,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then the smartest trap who ever swore a lying oath
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Could never foot me down the River track.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sweetbriar pricked her ears, and shook a foam flake from her bit,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As she heard his words, and doubtless caught their sense,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the rotten granite pebbles rattled round her as she lit
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the homeward side the Rosedale boundן¿½ry fence,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As they scrambled round by Lockerן¿½s-Hill, Jack Corrigan looked round,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as he looked was filled with stern delight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For he saw the baldfaced chestnut struggling fiercely on the ground,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though the hill shut out the sequel from his sight;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His triumph was but short, for, as he stemmed the wide morass,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where floods had muddied waters once so clear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And left the giant tussocks tangled tightly in a mass,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The trooper still kept drawing on his rear;

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Murrumbidgeeן¿½s icy stream was widened out by flood;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They swam it at the willow-shaded ford,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As they passed the station buildings his long spurs were red with blood,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sweetbriarן¿½s heaving flanks were deeply scored.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her stride grew more uneven, though she answered every call,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No jockey rode a better race than Jack
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As he eased her up the hills and pressed her onward down the fall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Round the sidlings of the Billylingra track.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They left Oן¿½Rourkeן¿½s behind them, where it fronts the big bald hill,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At the Flat Rock Jack was riding all he knew,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With all the dash and judgement of the famed Monaro skill,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet he couldnן¿½t keep the trooper out of view;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spied his tiny homestead as Bill Fraser gained apace
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And loudly warned the fugitive to yield,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who turned half round but saw no sign of pity in his face
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As they swept across the cultivation field;

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their hoofsן¿½ dull thunder brought the wife in wonder to the gate,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She waved her hand in answer to his shout;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While Dandy from his paddock whinnied loudly to his mate,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To know what all the trouble was about.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½God help us now, the end has come!ן¿½ the wretched woman cried,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And leant against the gate to catch her breath;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While the tiny, blue-eyed toddler cheered his father on his ride
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Towards the ghastly winning-post of Death.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The fillyן¿½s failing fast,ן¿½ thought Jack; ן¿½sheן¿½s nothing but a weed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Itן¿½s a certainty she canן¿½t keep long in front.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ll make a splendid target, if he likes to draw a bead,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As I try to cross the river on the punt.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He left the mare and scrambled through the ti-tree growing rank,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Deep rooted in its bed of yellow clay,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But when he reached the river, stood and trembled on the bank,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½My God!ן¿½ he hoarsely said, ן¿½itן¿½s swept away!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The punt was gone, the rope of wire still stretched from shore to shore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Jack paused but half a moment to decide,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as he scrambled down the bank the wondן¿½ring trooper saw
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Him struggling half across the rushing tide,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The angry waters swept him down, and every nerve was strained
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To keep his hold upon the frail support,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though icy numbness seized him, yet his courage never waned,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The hope of freedom filled his every thought.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The rope swayed low beneath his weight and bellied to the stream,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Around his head the flying ripples curled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While high above the riverן¿½s roar rang out the awful scream
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of a soul that flies in terror from the world.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A mighty log, borne swiftly on the bosom of the flood,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Resistless swept him ן¿½neath the eager wave,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sucked him down to river depths, and there beneath the foam,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Jack Corrigan sought out a nameless grave,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Good-bye to life, good-bye to life,ן¿½ the mocking wavelets sang,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The towering cliffs took up the wild refrain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A broken life, a weeping wife,ן¿½ ן¿½Twas thus the rhythm rang,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And a baby boy heן¿½ll never see again.ן¿½
meaning:
Barcroft Boake's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "Jack Corrigan"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
